title: Herbal beeswax pillar candle
subtitle: A beeswax pillar candle with dried herbs pressed decoratively into the outer surface
excerpt: Dried herbs pressed into the warm outer surface of a beeswax pillar candle give it a beautifully botanical look. The herbs are purely decorative and positioned on the outer surface only, never inside the wax where they could create a fire path during burning.
sourceNotes: Beeswax pillar candle method from standard candle-making references. Decorative herb pressing timing from Soap Queen and homesteading candle guides.

recipe.yieldDescription: One pillar candle (approximately 7 cm x 10 cm) from 250 g beeswax
recipe.batchNotes: Each candle requires individual herb pressing at the right window; difficult to batch.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Can be made weeks ahead. The herbs hold well as long as the candle is stored away from high humidity.
recipe.temperatureNote: Pour beeswax at 70 to 75 degrees Celsius. Press herbs 10 minutes after pouring when the outer surface is firm but the centre is still warm.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: 5 years or more. Beeswax is exceptionally stable. Dried herbs may fade in colour but hold their shape.

recipeTools.notes: Polycarbonate or aluminium; lightly sprayed with release agent
To hold wick centred in mould

glossaryTerms.definition: A free-standing, unmoulded candle that does not require a container to hold its shape. Pillar candles require a harder wax than container candles; beeswax and paraffin are commonly used.
A natural wax secreted by honeybees, with a melting point of 62 to 65 degrees Celsius, a characteristic honey scent, and a longer burn time than most other waxes.

A light spray or coating applied to the inside of a rigid candle mould to help the set candle slide out cleanly. A very light spray of cooking oil or dedicated silicone release spray works well for beeswax.
The white powdery film that can form on beeswax candle surfaces over time, caused by migration of fatty acids. Harmless and removable with a soft cloth.

A channel or material inside a candle that connects the wick's flame to combustible material beyond the normal melt pool. Organic matter (herbs, petals) embedded inside the wax body can act as a fire path and must be avoided.

content.text: is harder and longer-burning than one made with soy. Pressing dried herbs into the warm outer surface adds a handmade botanical quality. The critical rule is that herbs must remain on the surface only: embedding them inside the body creates a potential
to the combustible material. The pressing window opens approximately 10 minutes after pouring and closes as the wax solidifies. Beeswax may develop a white
over time; buff gently with a soft cloth. Shelf life is 5 years or more.

items.prepNote: Sized for the mould diameter; consult wick sizing guide
Lavender sprigs, rosemary, chamomile flowers, or thyme; fully dried
Very light application; too much will leave an oily residue on the candle surface

content.text: to the inside of the pillar mould. Thread the wick through the hole in the base of the mould and centre it using a wick rod across the top. Seal the wick hole at the base with a blob of mould sealer or Blu Tack to prevent leaks.

Melt beeswax in a double boiler to 72 to 75 degrees Celsius. Pour carefully into the mould. After approximately 10 minutes the outer surface will begin to firm while the centre stays warm. At this point, gently press individual herb sprigs into the outer surface of the wax. Press firmly enough to anchor them but do not push through to the candle's core.

As the candle cools it will contract slightly, creating a depression in the centre. Pierce the surface with a skewer and top up with a second small pour. Leave to set for at least 3 hours before unmoulding. Trim the wick to 5 mm.

Pillar candles keep for 5 years or more. Store upright in a cool, dry place away from direct sunlight, which can fade the herbs. Trim the wick to 5 mm before each burn. Burn on a heat-safe plate and never leave unattended. Do not burn until the wax melts to reach the herb-decorated surface layer.
